name: Shakespeare's Plays
description: Classify Shakespeare's plays by genre and identify key characters and quotes

questions.question: Is Shakespeare's 'Hamlet' a comedy, tragedy, or history?
questions.explanation: Hamlet is a tragedy written around 1599-1601, set in the royal court of Elsinore Castle in Denmark. The key characters are Prince Hamlet, his uncle King Claudius, Queen Gertrude, Ophelia, and Polonius. The ghost of Hamlet's murdered father reveals that Claudius poisoned him to seize the throne and marry Gertrude; Hamlet feigns madness while plotting revenge, but his indecision leads to a catastrophic finale in which nearly every major character dies. The play contains the most famous line in English literature -- "To be, or not to be, that is the question" -- along with "Something is rotten in the state of Denmark" and "Good night, sweet prince." Based on a Norse legend recorded by Saxo Grammaticus and retold by Francois de Belleforest, it is Shakespeare's longest play at around 4,042 lines. Major film adaptations include Laurence Olivier's 1948 Best Picture winner, Franco Zeffirelli's 1990 version with Mel Gibson, and Kenneth Branagh's 1996 uncut four-hour adaptation. It is the most frequently performed Shakespeare play worldwide and has been translated into every major language.

questions.question: Is Shakespeare's 'Macbeth' a comedy, tragedy, or history?
questions.explanation: Macbeth is a tragedy first performed around 1606, set in medieval Scotland. The key characters are Macbeth, Lady Macbeth, Banquo, Macduff, and the Three Witches. Spurred by a prophecy from the witches and goaded by his wife, the Scottish general Macbeth murders King Duncan to seize the throne, then descends into paranoia and tyranny, ordering more killings to secure his power until he is slain by Macduff. Famous lines include "Is this a dagger which I see before me," "Out, damned spot," and "Double, double toil and trouble." Based on Raphael Holinshed's Chronicles of England, Scotland, and Ireland, it is Shakespeare's shortest tragedy at about 2,100 lines. Actors superstitiously call it "The Scottish Play" because saying the name inside a theater is considered bad luck. Notable film adaptations include Akira Kurosawa's Throne of Blood (1957), Roman Polanski's Macbeth (1971), and Joel Coen's The Tragedy of Macbeth (2021) with Denzel Washington. The play contains the most allusions to King James I, patron of Shakespeare's company, including its focus on Scottish royalty and witchcraft.

questions.question: Is Shakespeare's 'Othello' a comedy, tragedy, or history?
questions.explanation: Othello is a tragedy written around 1603, set in Venice and the military outpost of Cyprus. The key characters are Othello (a Moorish general in Venetian service), his wife Desdemona, his treacherous ensign Iago, Cassio, and Emilia. Iago, passed over for promotion, manipulates Othello into believing Desdemona is unfaithful with Cassio; consumed by jealousy, Othello smothers Desdemona before learning the truth and killing himself. Famous lines include "O, beware, my lord, of jealousy; it is the green-eyed monster," "Put out the light, and then put out the light," and Othello's anguished "Of one that loved not wisely, but too well." Based on Cinthio's 1565 Italian tale "Un Capitano Moro" from Hecatommithi. Samuel Taylor Coleridge described Iago's evil as "motiveless malignity." Iago speaks more lines than Othello himself despite not being the title character. Major adaptations include Orson Welles' 1951 film, Oliver Parker's 1995 version with Laurence Fishburne (the first African American to play Othello on film), and the modernized O (2001). Verdi's opera Otello (1887) is considered one of the greatest operas ever composed.

questions.question: Is Shakespeare's 'King Lear' a comedy, tragedy, or history?
questions.explanation: King Lear is a tragedy written in late 1605 or early 1606, set in pre-Roman Britain. The key characters are King Lear, his daughters Goneril, Regan, and Cordelia, the Earl of Gloucester, and Edgar. The aging Lear divides his kingdom among his daughters based on their flattery, banishing the honest Cordelia; Goneril and Regan betray him, driving him to madness on a stormy heath before a war brings tragic deaths including Cordelia's hanging. Famous lines include "Nothing will come of nothing," "Blow, winds, and crack your cheeks," and "As flies to wanton boys are we to the gods." Based on the legend of Leir of Britain from Geoffrey of Monmouth's Historia Regum Britanniae and an anonymous earlier play, The True Chronicle History of King Leir. The play was considered so bleak that for 150 years (1681-1838) theaters performed only Nahum Tate's rewritten version with a happy ending. Major film adaptations include Peter Brook's 1971 version, Akira Kurosawa's Ran (1985) which transposes the story to feudal Japan, and the 2018 BBC television film with Anthony Hopkins. Many critics consider it Shakespeare's greatest achievement.

questions.question: Is Shakespeare's 'Romeo and Juliet' a comedy, tragedy, or history?
questions.explanation: Romeo and Juliet is a tragedy written around 1594-1596, set in Verona and Mantua, Italy. The key characters are Romeo Montague, Juliet Capulet, Mercutio, Tybalt, Friar Laurence, and the Nurse. Two teenagers from feuding families fall in love at a masked ball, secretly marry, but a chain of street brawls, banishment, and a botched sleeping-potion plan leads to both lovers' suicides, which finally reconciles their families. Famous lines include "O Romeo, Romeo, wherefore art thou Romeo?," "A plague on both your houses," and "Parting is such sweet sorrow." Based on Arthur Brooke's 1562 narrative poem The Tragical History of Romeus and Juliet, itself derived from Italian sources by Bandello and da Porto. The entire plot spans just five days. It was among Shakespeare's most popular plays during his lifetime and has never fallen out of fashion. Major adaptations include Franco Zeffirelli's 1968 film, Baz Luhrmann's modernized Romeo + Juliet (1996) with Leonardo DiCaprio, and the musical West Side Story (1957/1961/2021). The title characters are the archetypal young lovers in Western culture.

questions.question: Is Shakespeare's 'Julius Caesar' a comedy, tragedy, or history?
questions.explanation: Julius Caesar is a tragedy written and first performed in 1599, set in ancient Rome in 44 BC. The key characters are Julius Caesar, Brutus, Cassius, Mark Antony, and Octavius. A group of Roman senators, led by Brutus and Cassius, assassinate Caesar on the Ides of March to prevent tyranny; Mark Antony's brilliant funeral oration turns the public against the conspirators, leading to civil war and the suicides of Brutus and Cassius at the Battle of Philippi. Famous lines include "Et tu, Brute?," "Friends, Romans, countrymen, lend me your ears," and "Beware the Ides of March." Based primarily on Sir Thomas North's 1579 English translation of Plutarch's Parallel Lives. Despite being about a real historical figure, it is classified as a tragedy, not a history -- Shakespeare's histories deal exclusively with English kings. It was likely the first play performed at the Globe Theatre in 1599. Major film adaptations include Joseph L. Mankiewicz's 1953 version starring Marlon Brando as Antony and the 1970 Stuart Burge film with Charlton Heston. The play is one of Shakespeare's most studied works in schools worldwide.

questions.question: Is Shakespeare's 'Antony and Cleopatra' a comedy, tragedy, or history?
questions.explanation: Antony and Cleopatra is a tragedy first performed around 1607, set across the Roman Republic and Ptolemaic Egypt with scenes spanning Rome, Alexandria, Athens, Messina, and battlefields at sea. The key characters are Mark Antony, Cleopatra, Octavius Caesar, Enobarbus, and Charmian. The Roman general Antony, one of three rulers of the known world, is torn between his political duty in Rome and his passionate love for the Egyptian queen Cleopatra; after losing the naval Battle of Actium to Octavius, Antony falls on his sword upon a false report of Cleopatra's death, and she then kills herself with an asp rather than be paraded in Octavius's triumph. Famous lines include "Age cannot wither her, nor custom stale her infinite variety" and "I am dying, Egypt, dying." Based on Plutarch's Life of Mark Antony via North's translation. The play has 42 scenes -- more than any other Shakespeare play -- and shifts locations constantly. Notable adaptations include the 1963 Cleopatra starring Elizabeth Taylor and Richard Burton (though not a direct adaptation), and the 1974 TV production with Janet Suzman. It is Shakespeare's longest tragedy after Hamlet.

questions.question: Is Shakespeare's 'Coriolanus' a comedy, tragedy, or history?
questions.explanation: Coriolanus is a tragedy written between 1605 and 1608, set in Rome during the early days of the Roman Republic, around 490 BC. The key characters are Caius Marcius Coriolanus, his mother Volumnia, his wife Virgilia, the tribunes Brutus and Sicinius, and his rival Tullus Aufidius. The brilliant but arrogant Roman general Coriolanus, who despises the common people, is banished from Rome after refusing to humble himself for their votes; he allies with the enemy Volscians to march on Rome, but his mother's plea persuades him to spare the city, leading to his assassination by the Volscians. Famous lines include "There is a world elsewhere" and Volumnia's plea "Think you I am no stronger than my sex?" Based on Plutarch's Life of Coriolanus via North's translation. T.S. Eliot controversially declared it superior to Hamlet. Along with Antony and Cleopatra, it is one of Shakespeare's final tragedies. Ralph Fiennes directed and starred in a 2011 film adaptation set in a modern war zone. The play has been repeatedly adopted by both left-wing and right-wing political movements as commentary on democracy versus elite rule.

questions.question: Is Shakespeare's 'Titus Andronicus' a comedy, tragedy, or history?
questions.explanation: Titus Andronicus is a tragedy written between 1588 and 1593, making it Shakespeare's first tragedy and possibly his earliest play. Set in a fictionalized late Roman Empire, the key characters are the general Titus Andronicus, his daughter Lavinia, Tamora (Queen of the Goths), her lover Aaron the Moor, and the emperor Saturninus. The victorious general Titus returns from war with Gothic captives, sparking a cycle of increasingly horrific revenge: Lavinia is raped and mutilated, Titus's sons are murdered, and Titus ultimately kills Tamora's sons, bakes them into a pie, and serves it to her at a banquet before the remaining characters slaughter each other. Famous lines include "If one good deed in all my life I did, I do repent it from my very soul" (Aaron). Influenced by Seneca's tragedies and Ovid's tale of Philomela from the Metamorphoses, it was Shakespeare's attempt to emulate the blood-soaked revenge plays popular in the 1590s. With 14 killings, multiple mutilations, and cannibalism, it is Shakespeare's most violent play by far. Julie Taymor's Titus (1999) starring Anthony Hopkins is the most notable film adaptation. The play was hugely popular in Shakespeare's day but fell out of favor for centuries before a critical revival in the 20th century.

questions.question: Is Shakespeare's 'Timon of Athens' a comedy, tragedy, or history?
questions.explanation: Timon of Athens is a tragedy written around 1606, likely co-authored with Thomas Middleton, set in ancient Athens and the surrounding wilderness. The key characters are Timon, the philosopher Apemantus, Timon's steward Flavius, the general Alcibiades, and various parasitic flatterers. The wealthy and generous Timon lavishes gifts on friends and hangers-on until he is bankrupt; when he turns to them for help, every one refuses, transforming him into a bitter misanthrope who retreats to a cave, discovers gold, and uses it to fund the destruction of Athens before dying in the wilderness. Famous lines include "I am Misanthropos, and hate mankind" and his speech on gold: "Gold? Yellow, glittering, precious gold?" Based on Plutarch's Life of Antony (which mentions Timon) and Lucian's dialogue Timon the Misanthrope. Karl Marx quoted Timon's speeches about gold extensively in Das Kapital and Economic and Philosophic Manuscripts as a critique of money's corrupting power. The play is widely believed to be unfinished or a rough draft, which may explain why it was not performed during Shakespeare's lifetime. It remains one of Shakespeare's least performed plays, though a 2012 National Theatre production starring Simon Russell Beale won critical acclaim.

questions.question: Is Shakespeare's 'A Midsummer Night's Dream' a comedy, tragedy, or history?
questions.explanation: A Midsummer Night's Dream is a comedy written around 1595-1596, set in Athens and an enchanted forest nearby. The key characters are the fairy king Oberon, fairy queen Titania, the mischievous sprite Puck (Robin Goodfellow), the four young Athenian lovers (Hermia, Lysander, Helena, Demetrius), and Bottom the weaver. Three intertwining plots converge in a magical forest on the eve of Duke Theseus's wedding: four young lovers flee Athens and become entangled in a fairy love-potion mix-up, Bottom is transformed into a donkey-headed man and wooed by the enchanted Titania, and a troupe of amateur actors rehearse a hilariously bad play-within-a-play, "Pyramus and Thisbe." Famous lines include "Lord, what fools these mortals be!" and "The course of true love never did run smooth." Sources include Ovid's Metamorphoses, Chaucer's Knight's Tale, and English fairy folklore. Major adaptations include Max Reinhardt's 1935 film, Peter Hall's 1968 version, and Michael Hoffman's 1999 film with Kevin Kline. Benjamin Britten's 1960 opera is a landmark musical adaptation. It is one of Shakespeare's most frequently performed plays worldwide and the most popular choice for outdoor summer theater productions.

questions.question: Is Shakespeare's 'Much Ado About Nothing' a comedy, tragedy, or history?
questions.explanation: Much Ado About Nothing is a comedy written in 1598-1599, set in Messina, Sicily. The key characters are Benedick, Beatrice, Claudio, Hero, Don Pedro, and the villain Don John. Two plots interweave: the romantic Claudio is tricked by the villainous Don John into publicly shaming his innocent fiancee Hero at the altar, while the witty sparring partners Benedick and Beatrice are tricked by their friends into admitting they love each other. Famous lines include "I do love nothing in the world so well as you -- is not that strange?" and Benedick's "When I said I would die a bachelor, I did not think I should live till I were married." The title is likely a bawdy Elizabethan pun -- "nothing" was slang for female anatomy and was pronounced similarly to "noting" (eavesdropping), which drives the entire plot. Sources include Ariosto's Orlando Furioso and Bandello's Novelle. Kenneth Branagh's 1993 film is the definitive screen adaptation, and Joss Whedon directed a low-budget modern version in 2012. The Beatrice-Benedick dynamic has influenced every romantic comedy featuring bickering lovers who are secretly attracted to each other, from Pride and Prejudice to modern rom-coms.

questions.question: Is Shakespeare's 'Twelfth Night' a comedy, tragedy, or history?
questions.explanation: Twelfth Night is a romantic comedy written around 1601-1602, set in the fictional country of Illyria (based on the Adriatic coast). The key characters are Viola, her twin brother Sebastian, Duke Orsino, Countess Olivia, the steward Malvolio, and Sir Toby Belch. After a shipwreck separates twin siblings Viola and Sebastian, Viola disguises herself as a boy named Cesario and enters the service of Duke Orsino, whom she secretly loves; Orsino sends her to woo Countess Olivia on his behalf, but Olivia falls for the disguised Viola instead, creating a tangled love triangle resolved only when Sebastian appears. Famous lines include "If music be the food of love, play on," "Some are born great, some achieve greatness, and some have greatness thrust upon them," and "Be not afraid of greatness." Based on Barnabe Riche's tale "Of Apolonius and Silla" (1581), itself derived from Italian sources. In Shakespeare's time, a boy actor would play Viola -- a woman disguised as a man -- adding layers of gender comedy. The subtitle "What You Will" may be a throwaway or a philosophical joke. Trevor Nunn directed a 1996 film with Helena Bonham Carter, and the play inspired the 2006 teen film She's the Man. Named for the Twelfth Night holiday marking the end of Christmas festivities.

questions.question: Is Shakespeare's 'The Taming of the Shrew' a comedy, tragedy, or history?
questions.explanation: The Taming of the Shrew is a comedy written between 1590 and 1592, set in Padua, Italy, with an induction scene set in the English countryside. The key characters are Katherina (Kate, the "shrew"), Petruchio, her sister Bianca, Lucentio, and the drunken tinker Christopher Sly. A framing device has a nobleman trick the drunkard Sly into watching a play, in which the fortune-hunting Petruchio woos and "tames" the fiery Katherina through psychological manipulation -- starving her, denying her sleep, and contradicting reality -- while multiple suitors compete for her milder sister Bianca. Famous lines include Kate's controversial final speech: "Thy husband is thy lord, thy life, thy keeper." Sources include the anonymous play The Taming of a Shrew and Ariosto's I Suppositi. It has become one of Shakespeare's most controversial works due to its gender politics -- productions must decide whether Kate is genuinely tamed or playing along subversively. Cole Porter's musical Kiss Me, Kate (1948/1953 film) is the most famous adaptation. Franco Zeffirelli directed Elizabeth Taylor and Richard Burton in a 1967 film version. The Gil Junger teen film 10 Things I Hate About You (1999) is a modern retelling.

questions.question: Is Shakespeare's 'As You Like It' a comedy, tragedy, or history?
questions.explanation: As You Like It is a pastoral comedy written around 1599, set in the court of Duke Frederick and the Forest of Arden. The key characters are Rosalind, Orlando, Celia, Touchstone the fool, the melancholy Jaques, and the banished Duke Senior. After the usurping Duke Frederick banishes his niece Rosalind, she disguises herself as a young man named Ganymede and flees to the Forest of Arden with her cousin Celia, where she encounters the lovesick Orlando and playfully teaches him how to woo -- while he unknowingly courts the very woman he loves. Famous lines include the "All the world's a stage" speech ("And all the men and women merely players") and "Too much of a good thing." Based on Thomas Lodge's 1590 prose romance Rosalynde. The Forest of Arden is likely named after the Forest of Arden near Stratford-upon-Avon and possibly after Shakespeare's mother, Mary Arden. Rosalind has more lines than any other female character in Shakespeare. Kenneth Branagh directed a 2006 film adaptation set in 19th-century Japan. The play contains Shakespeare's most direct reference to the theater world: Jaques's "seven ages of man" speech is essentially a theatrical metaphor for human life.

questions.question: Is Shakespeare's 'The Merchant of Venice' a comedy, tragedy, or history?
questions.explanation: The Merchant of Venice is technically a comedy, written between 1596 and 1598, set in Venice and Belmont, Italy. The key characters are Shylock (a Jewish moneylender), Antonio (the merchant), Bassanio, Portia, and Jessica (Shylock's daughter). Antonio borrows money from Shylock to fund his friend Bassanio's courtship of the wealthy Portia, agreeing to forfeit a pound of flesh if he defaults; when Antonio's ships are lost, Portia disguises herself as a lawyer and defeats Shylock with the argument that he may take flesh but not a drop of blood. Famous lines include "Hath not a Jew eyes?," "The quality of mercy is not strained," and "All that glisters is not gold." Based on Giovanni Fiorentino's Il Pecorone (1558) and possibly Christopher Marlowe's The Jew of Malta. Though classified as a comedy, the play is deeply uncomfortable for modern audiences due to Shylock's forced conversion to Christianity. The "pound of flesh" has become a universal metaphor for a cruel legal demand. Al Pacino starred as Shylock in Michael Radford's 2004 film. The play is sometimes reclassified as a "problem play" alongside Measure for Measure and All's Well That Ends Well.

questions.question: Is Shakespeare's 'The Merry Wives of Windsor' a comedy, tragedy, or history?
questions.explanation: The Merry Wives of Windsor is a comedy written in or before 1597, set in the town of Windsor, England -- making it one of the very few Shakespeare plays set in contemporary England rather than a distant time or place. The key characters are Sir John Falstaff, Mistress Ford, Mistress Page, Master Ford, and Anne Page. The fat, broke knight Falstaff sends identical love letters to two married middle-class women hoping to seduce them for their money; the women discover the scheme and team up to humiliate him repeatedly, culminating in a midnight prank in Windsor Forest involving fairies and pinching. Famous lines include "Why, then the world's mine oyster" and Falstaff's comic misadventures. Legend holds that Queen Elizabeth I so loved Falstaff in Henry IV that she commanded Shakespeare to write a play showing him in love, supposedly completed in just two weeks. Based loosely on Italian novella traditions but largely original. The Falstaff of this play is notably less witty than in Henry IV, leading some scholars to view it as a pale imitation. It has been adapted into at least ten operas, most famously Verdi's Falstaff (1893), widely regarded as one of the greatest comic operas. Otto Nicolai's The Merry Wives of Windsor (1849) is another beloved operatic version.

questions.question: Is Shakespeare's 'The Tempest' a comedy, tragedy, or history?
questions.explanation: The Tempest is a romance (or late comedy) written around 1610-1611, set on a remote, enchanted island. The key characters are Prospero (a sorcerer and the rightful Duke of Milan), his daughter Miranda, the spirit Ariel, the savage Caliban, and the usurping brother Antonio. Prospero, deposed twelve years earlier by his brother and set adrift, has mastered magic on the island; he conjures a tempest to shipwreck his enemies on the shore, orchestrates the romance between Miranda and Ferdinand (son of his enemy the King of Naples), and ultimately forgives his betrayers and renounces his magical powers. Famous lines include "We are such stuff as dreams are made on," "O brave new world, that has such people in't," and "Our revels now are ended." It is believed to be the last play Shakespeare wrote alone, and Prospero's farewell speech is widely read as Shakespeare's own farewell to the theater. Sources include accounts of the 1609 Sea Venture shipwreck in Bermuda and Montaigne's essay "Of Cannibals." Major adaptations include the science-fiction film Forbidden Planet (1956), Peter Greenaway's Prospero's Books (1991) with John Gielgud, and Julie Taymor's 2010 version with Helen Mirren as a gender-swapped Prospera. Aldous Huxley took the title Brave New World from Miranda's line.

questions.question: Is Shakespeare's 'The Comedy of Errors' a comedy, tragedy, or history?
questions.explanation: The Comedy of Errors is a farce written around 1592-1594, set in Ephesus (ancient Greece). The key characters are the two Antipholuses (twin masters from Syracuse and Ephesus), the two Dromios (their twin servants), and Adriana (wife of Antipholus of Ephesus). Two sets of identical twins, separated at birth by a shipwreck, end up in the same city, causing escalating chaos as wives, merchants, courtesans, and officers mistake one twin for the other, leading to accusations of theft, infidelity, madness, and demonic possession before a joyful reunion. Famous lines include "Am I in earth, in heaven, or in hell? Sleeping or waking, mad or well-advised?" Based on Plautus's Roman comedies Menaechmi and Amphitruo, making it Shakespeare's most directly classical adaptation. At roughly 1,786 lines, it is Shakespeare's shortest play of any genre. Shakespeare doubled Plautus's single set of twins to two sets, exponentially multiplying the confusion. The play's title has entered the English language as an idiom meaning an event made absurd by accumulated errors. Rodgers and Hart adapted it as the musical The Boys from Syracuse (1938). It was one of Shakespeare's earliest plays and demonstrates his command of classical comic structure from the very start of his career.

questions.question: Is Shakespeare's 'Love's Labour's Lost' a comedy, tragedy, or history?
questions.explanation: Love's Labour's Lost is a comedy written in the mid-1590s, set in the Kingdom of Navarre in southwestern France. The key characters are the King of Navarre, his three lords Berowne, Longaville, and Dumaine, the Princess of France, and her ladies Rosaline, Maria, and Katherine. The King and his companions swear to devote three years to study and fasting, forswearing the company of women, but immediately break their oaths when the Princess of France and her attendants arrive on a diplomatic mission, leading to elaborate courtship, disguises, and wordplay. The play is highly unusual for a comedy in that it does not end with a marriage -- the Princess's father dies and the women impose a year's penance on the men before accepting them. Famous lines include "A jest's prosperity lies in the ear of him that hears it" and Berowne's defense of love over study. It has no known direct source -- one of the few Shakespeare plays that appears to be entirely original. Shakespeare reportedly wrote a sequel called Love's Labour's Won, which is now lost and is one of the great mysteries of English literature. Kenneth Branagh directed a 2000 musical film adaptation set in the 1930s. The play is among Shakespeare's most linguistically dense, packed with puns, sonnets, and rhetorical set pieces.

questions.question: Is Shakespeare's 'The Two Gentlemen of Verona' a comedy, tragedy, or history?
questions.explanation: The Two Gentlemen of Verona is a comedy written between 1589 and 1593, set in Verona, Milan, and a forest near Mantua. The key characters are the two gentlemen Valentine and Proteus, the women Silvia and Julia, and the clownish servant Launce with his dog Crab. Best friends Valentine and Proteus both fall in love with Silvia, the Duke of Milan's daughter; the treacherous Proteus betrays Valentine (who is banished and becomes leader of outlaws) while abandoning his own faithful Julia, who follows him disguised as a boy. Famous lines include "Who is Silvia? What is she, that all our swains commend her?" (set to music by Schubert). Based on the story of Felix and Felismena from Jorge de Montemayor's pastoral romance Diana (1559). Considered by many scholars to be Shakespeare's earliest play, it introduced themes he would revisit throughout his career: cross-dressing heroines, banished lovers, outlaws in forests, and the conflict between love and friendship. The dog Crab has been called "the most scene-stealing non-speaking role in the canon." The problematic ending, where Valentine forgives Proteus and apparently offers Silvia to him, has troubled audiences for centuries. It is one of Shakespeare's least performed plays.

questions.question: Is Shakespeare's 'Henry V' a comedy, tragedy, or history?
questions.explanation: Henry V is a history play written around 1599, set in England and France during the Hundred Years' War, specifically around the 1415 Battle of Agincourt. The key characters are King Henry V, the French Dauphin, the Earl of Exeter, Fluellen, and Princess Katherine of France. The newly matured King Henry -- the wild Prince Hal of the Henry IV plays -- invades France to press his claim to the French throne, delivers a rousing speech before the vastly outnumbered English army wins a stunning victory at Agincourt, and woos the French princess to seal peace. Famous lines include "Once more unto the breach, dear friends" and the St. Crispin's Day speech: "We few, we happy few, we band of brothers." Based on Holinshed's Chronicles and Edward Hall's The Union of the Two Noble Families. The play is the final installment of Shakespeare's second Henriad tetralogy (Richard II, Henry IV Parts 1-2, Henry V). Laurence Olivier's 1944 film was made as wartime propaganda and dedicated to the British commandos; Kenneth Branagh's gritty 1989 version offered a more anti-war interpretation. It uniquely features a Chorus narrator who repeatedly apologizes for the limitations of the stage. The play was likely performed at the newly built Globe Theatre in its first season.

questions.question: Is Shakespeare's 'Richard III' a comedy, tragedy, or history?
questions.explanation: Richard III is a history play written around 1592-1593, set in England during the Wars of the Roses and the brief reign of Richard III (1483-1485). The key characters are Richard (Duke of Gloucester, then King), Queen Margaret, the Duchess of York, Lady Anne, and the Duke of Buckingham. The deformed, brilliant Richard schemes his way to the throne through murder, manipulation, and seduction -- having his own brother Clarence drowned in a barrel of wine and the young princes smothered in the Tower of London -- before being defeated and killed by Henry Tudor at the Battle of Bosworth Field. Famous lines include "Now is the winter of our discontent," "A horse! A horse! My kingdom for a horse!" and "Was ever woman in this humour wooed?" Based on Thomas More's History of Richard III and Holinshed's Chronicles. Shakespeare's portrayal of Richard as a hunchbacked villain is Tudor propaganda; when Richard's actual skeleton was discovered under a Leicester parking lot in 2012, it confirmed the scoliosis but not the monstrous characterization. The title role is one of the longest in Shakespeare and a career-defining part for actors. Laurence Olivier's 1955 film is iconic, Ian McKellen starred in a 1995 fascist-era adaptation, and Al Pacino explored the role in the documentary Looking for Richard (1996).

questions.question: Is Shakespeare's 'Henry IV, Part 1' a comedy, tragedy, or history?
questions.explanation: Henry IV, Part 1 is a history play written in the mid-1590s and first published in 1598, set in England around 1402-1403. The key characters are King Henry IV, Prince Hal (the future Henry V), Sir John Falstaff, Hotspur (Henry Percy), and Owen Glendower. The guilt-ridden King Henry faces a rebellion led by the fiery Hotspur while his son Prince Hal wastes time drinking and carousing in Eastcheap taverns with the lovable rogue Falstaff; Hal ultimately redeems himself by killing Hotspur in single combat at the Battle of Shrewsbury. Famous lines include Falstaff's "The better part of valour is discretion" and "Banish plump Jack, and banish all the world." Based on Holinshed's Chronicles and the anonymous play The Famous Victories of Henry the Fifth. The play introduced Falstaff, who became arguably Shakespeare's most beloved comic creation -- Harold Bloom called him Shakespeare's greatest invention and a character who rivals Hamlet in depth. Falstaff was originally named Sir John Oldcastle, but the name was changed after Oldcastle's descendants (the powerful Cobham family) objected. It was one of Shakespeare's most popular plays in his lifetime, going through six quarto editions. The Orson Welles film Chimes at Midnight (1965) brilliantly combines both Henry IV plays from Falstaff's perspective.

questions.question: Is Shakespeare's 'Henry IV, Part 2' a comedy, tragedy, or history?
questions.explanation: Henry IV, Part 2 is a history play written between 1596 and 1599, set in England during the final years of Henry IV's reign. The key characters are King Henry IV, Prince Hal, Falstaff, the Lord Chief Justice, Mistress Quickly, and Doll Tearsheet. With the rebellion still simmering after Shrewsbury, the aging and sick King Henry worries about his dissolute heir; Falstaff recruits soldiers corruptly and carouses through the countryside, while Hal gradually prepares for kingship, culminating in his coronation and the devastating rejection of Falstaff: "I know thee not, old man." Famous lines include the king's lament "Uneasy lies the head that wears a crown" and Falstaff's reflection "We have heard the chimes at midnight." Based on Holinshed's Chronicles. The play is darker and more melancholic than Part 1, with the aging Falstaff losing his comic vitality. Hal's public rejection of Falstaff is one of the most emotionally devastating moments in all of Shakespeare -- audiences still debate whether it is a necessary act of royal maturity or an act of cruelty. It is the third play in the second Henriad tetralogy (Richard II, Henry IV Parts 1-2, Henry V). Falstaff became so popular that Shakespeare supposedly wrote The Merry Wives of Windsor as a spin-off at the Queen's request.

questions.question: Is Shakespeare's 'Richard II' a comedy, tragedy, or history?
questions.explanation: Richard II is a history play written around 1595, set in England during the final years of Richard II's reign (1398-1400). The key characters are King Richard II, Henry Bolingbroke (the future Henry IV), John of Gaunt, the Duke of York, and Queen Isabel. The poetic but politically inept Richard banishes Bolingbroke and seizes his inheritance; while Richard is away fighting in Ireland, Bolingbroke returns with an army, and Richard is forced to abdicate, imprisoned in Pontefract Castle, and murdered. Famous lines include John of Gaunt's deathbed speech "This royal throne of kings, this sceptred isle" and Richard's "For God's sake, let us sit upon the ground and tell sad stories of the death of kings." Based on Holinshed's Chronicles and possibly Samuel Daniel's poem The Civil Wars. The play is written entirely in verse -- it contains no prose whatsoever, unique among Shakespeare's plays. Queen Elizabeth I reportedly said "I am Richard II, know ye not that?" believing the play commented on her reign and the danger of deposition. The Earl of Essex's supporters commissioned a performance the night before his 1601 rebellion, hoping to incite the public. It is the first play in the second Henriad tetralogy. Ben Whishaw starred in a 2012 BBC adaptation as part of The Hollow Crown series.

questions.question: Is Shakespeare's 'Henry VI, Part 1' a comedy, tragedy, or history?
questions.explanation: Henry VI, Part 1 is a history play written around 1591, set in England and France during the early reign of the child-king Henry VI and the later stages of the Hundred Years' War. The key characters are King Henry VI, Lord Talbot, Joan of Arc (called Joan la Pucelle), the Duke of Gloucester, and the Duke of York. The play dramatizes England's losses in France under the weak King Henry, the heroic English commander Talbot's battles against Joan of Arc, and the first stirrings of the Wars of the Roses as the houses of York and Lancaster begin their fatal rivalry in the Temple Garden scene. Famous lines include the Temple Garden scene where nobles pluck red and white roses to signal their allegiances. Based on Holinshed's Chronicles and Edward Hall's chronicle. It was probably co-written with Thomas Nashe and possibly other collaborators. Joan of Arc is portrayed unsympathetically as a witch and fraud -- a stark contrast to her modern sainthood. The play was enormously popular when first performed, reportedly drawing 10,000 spectators over its initial run at the Rose Theatre. It is the first play in Shakespeare's first tetralogy of history plays (Henry VI Parts 1-3, Richard III), which covers the Wars of the Roses.

questions.question: Is Shakespeare's 'Henry VIII' a comedy, tragedy, or history?
questions.explanation: Henry VIII is a history play written around 1612-1613, co-authored with John Fletcher (Shakespeare's collaborator and successor as principal playwright of the King's Men). Set in the English court during the reign of Henry VIII, the key characters are King Henry VIII, Cardinal Wolsey, Queen Katherine of Aragon, Anne Boleyn, and Archbishop Cranmer. The play dramatizes the fall of the powerful Cardinal Wolsey, the annulment of Henry's marriage to Katherine, his marriage to Anne Boleyn, and the christening of the future Elizabeth I, ending on a prophetic note celebrating England's glorious future. Famous lines include Wolsey's farewell: "Had I but served my God with half the zeal I served my king, he would not in mine age have left me naked to mine enemies." Based on Holinshed's Chronicles and George Cavendish's Life of Wolsey. Originally titled All Is True, as recorded in contemporary documents. The play is historically significant because during a performance on June 29, 1613, a theatrical cannon set fire to the Globe Theatre's thatched roof and burned it to the ground -- no one was killed, though reportedly one man's breeches caught fire and were extinguished with a bottle of ale. It has more stage directions than any other Shakespeare play, reflecting its elaborate pageantry and spectacle.

questions.question: Is Shakespeare's 'King John' a comedy, tragedy, or history?
questions.explanation: King John is a history play written in the mid-1590s, set in England and France during the reign of King John (1199-1216). The key characters are King John, his mother Eleanor of Aquitaine, the Bastard Philip Faulconbridge, Constance of Brittany, and the young Prince Arthur. The play follows John's troubled reign as he faces challenges from France, the Pope, and rival claimants to the throne, particularly his nephew Arthur, whose imprisonment and death haunt the rest of the play; John is eventually poisoned by a monk and succeeded by his son Henry III. Famous lines include the Bastard's closing speech: "This England never did, nor never shall, lie at the proud foot of a conqueror" and "Mad world! Mad kings! Mad composition!" Based on the anonymous two-part play The Troublesome Reign of King John (c. 1589) and Holinshed's Chronicles. Surprisingly, Shakespeare's King John never once mentions the Magna Carta -- the most famous event of John's actual reign. The Bastard Faulconbridge, an illegitimate son of Richard the Lionheart, is Shakespeare's invention and serves as the play's moral compass. It is one of Shakespeare's least performed histories, though its themes of political legitimacy and contested succession resonate in every era. Herbert Beerbohm Tree produced a famous lavish staging in 1899.

questions.question: In which Shakespeare play does the character Puck appear?
questions.explanation: Puck, also called Robin Goodfellow, appears in A Midsummer Night's Dream (c. 1595-1596), a comedy set in Athens and a nearby enchanted forest. Puck is a mischievous fairy servant to Oberon, King of the Fairies, who sends him to fetch a magical flower whose juice causes people to fall in love with the first thing they see. Puck mistakenly applies it to the wrong Athenian lover, causing romantic chaos, and also transforms the weaver Bottom's head into that of a donkey for his own amusement. His famous closing speech -- "If we shadows have offended, think but this, and all is mended" -- breaks the fourth wall and asks the audience's forgiveness. The character draws on English folklore traditions of Robin Goodfellow, a household sprite known for pranks. Puck's line "Lord, what fools these mortals be!" is one of Shakespeare's most quoted. In the fairy hierarchy, Puck is not royalty but a jester-servant, making him the comic trickster archetype. Stanley Tucci voiced Puck in the 1999 film. The character has become synonymous with playful mischief in English, and "puckish" entered the dictionary meaning impish or whimsical.

questions.question: In which Shakespeare play does the character Shylock appear?
questions.explanation: Shylock appears in The Merchant of Venice (c. 1596-1598), a comedy set in Venice and Belmont, Italy. Shylock is a Jewish moneylender who agrees to lend 3,000 ducats to the merchant Antonio with the condition that if the loan is not repaid in three months, Shylock may take a pound of Antonio's flesh. When Antonio defaults, Shylock insists on the bond in court, but Portia, disguised as a young lawyer, defeats him by ruling he may take flesh but not a single drop of blood, and he is further stripped of his wealth and forced to convert to Christianity. His speech "Hath not a Jew eyes? Hath not a Jew hands, organs, dimensions, senses, affections, passions?" is one of Shakespeare's most powerful pleas for common humanity. The character has been interpreted across centuries as everything from a comic villain to a tragic victim of antisemitism. Al Pacino played Shylock in the 2004 film and in a 2010 stage production. The phrase "a pound of flesh" has entered English as a metaphor for a harsh but legally binding demand. Whether Shylock is a villain or a sympathetic figure remains one of the most debated questions in Shakespeare criticism.

questions.question: In which Shakespeare play does the character Prospero appear?
questions.explanation: Prospero appears in The Tempest (c. 1610-1611), a romance set on a remote enchanted island. Prospero is the rightful Duke of Milan who was deposed by his brother Antonio twelve years before the play begins, set adrift with his infant daughter Miranda, and washed ashore on a magical island where he mastered sorcery, enslaved the spirit Ariel and the creature Caliban, and bided his time until he could conjure a storm to bring his enemies to shore. He orchestrates the entire plot -- including the romance between Miranda and Ferdinand -- before forgiving his betrayers and renouncing magic in the famous epilogue: "Now my charms are all o'erthrown, and what strength I have's mine own." The character is widely interpreted as Shakespeare's self-portrait, with Prospero's renunciation of magic mirroring Shakespeare's retirement from the theater. His speech "Our revels now are ended. These our actors, as I foretold you, were all spirits" is perhaps the most meta-theatrical moment in the canon. The name Prospero means "fortunate" or "prosperous" in Italian. John Gielgud played the role memorably in Peter Greenaway's Prospero's Books (1991), and Helen Mirren played a gender-swapped Prospera in Julie Taymor's 2010 film.

questions.question: In which Shakespeare history play does the character Falstaff first appear?
questions.explanation: Sir John Falstaff first appears in Henry IV, Part 1 (c. mid-1590s), a history play set in England around 1402-1403. Falstaff is the fat, witty, cowardly, dishonest, and utterly charming knight who serves as Prince Hal's companion in the taverns of Eastcheap, providing comic counterpoint to the political drama of rebellion. He appears in Henry IV Parts 1 and 2, The Merry Wives of Windsor, and his death is reported in Henry V. Falstaff was originally named Sir John Oldcastle, after a real historical figure, but Shakespeare changed the name after Oldcastle's descendants -- the powerful Cobham family -- objected, and a disclaimer was added to the epilogue of Henry IV, Part 2. Harold Bloom called Falstaff "the greatest wit in all of literature" and argued he changed human consciousness. His most famous lines include "The better part of valour is discretion," "Banish plump Jack, and banish all the world," and his catechism on honor: "What is honour? A word." Orson Welles considered Falstaff so important that he built his film Chimes at Midnight (1965) around him, calling it his finest work. Verdi's opera Falstaff (1893), based on The Merry Wives of Windsor, is considered one of the greatest comic operas ever written. The character has become the archetype of the lovable rogue in English literature.

questions.question: In which Shakespeare play does the villain Iago appear?
questions.explanation: Iago appears in Othello (c. 1603), a tragedy set in Venice and Cyprus. Iago is Othello's ensign (standard-bearer) who orchestrates the Moor's destruction out of resentment -- possibly because he was passed over for promotion in favor of Cassio, possibly out of racial hatred, possibly out of suspected cuckolding, or possibly from pure evil. He manipulates Othello into believing his wife Desdemona is unfaithful by planting a handkerchief as false evidence, driving Othello to murder her. Iago has the most lines of any character in the play (1,097) despite not being the title character, dominating the action through soliloquies that reveal his scheming to the audience. Samuel Taylor Coleridge famously described Iago's evil as "the motive-hunting of motiveless malignity," suggesting his stated reasons are rationalizations for a deeper, inexplicable wickedness. His final line -- "Demand me nothing. What you know, you know. From this time forth I never will speak word" -- is one of literature's most chilling refusals of explanation. Kenneth Branagh played Iago opposite Laurence Fishburne in the 1995 film. The character has been called the greatest villain in English literature, rivaled only by Milton's Satan in Paradise Lost. Disney named the villainous parrot in Aladdin (1992) after him.

questions.question: Which Shakespeare character delivers the 'To be, or not to be' soliloquy?
questions.explanation: Prince Hamlet delivers the "To be, or not to be" soliloquy in Act 3, Scene 1 of Hamlet (c. 1599-1601), a tragedy set in Elsinore Castle, Denmark. It is the most famous soliloquy in Western literature and possibly the most quoted passage in the English language. Hamlet meditates on whether it is nobler to endure life's suffering or to end it through death -- "to die, to sleep; to sleep, perchance to dream -- ay, there's the rub" -- concluding that fear of the unknown afterlife ("the undiscovered country from whose bourn no traveller returns") makes cowards of us all. The speech is 35 lines long and appears at the play's midpoint, when Hamlet is being watched by Claudius and Polonius who are spying from behind a curtain. Scholars debate whether Hamlet is genuinely contemplating suicide or philosophizing abstractly about human existence. The phrase "To be, or not to be" has been translated into virtually every language and parodied countless times. Every major actor who has played Hamlet must put their stamp on this speech -- notable interpretations include Laurence Olivier (1948), Kenneth Branagh (1996), David Tennant (2009), and Benedict Cumberbatch (2015). The speech also contains the origin of the common phrase "there's the rub," borrowed from the game of bowls.
questions.cardFront: To be, or not to be

questions.question: Which Shakespeare character says 'Friends, Romans, countrymen, lend me your ears'?
questions.explanation: Mark Antony delivers the "Friends, Romans, countrymen" funeral oration in Act 3, Scene 2 of Julius Caesar (1599), a tragedy set in ancient Rome in 44 BC. The speech is considered the greatest example of rhetorical persuasion in English literature. After Brutus addresses the Roman crowd explaining why Caesar had to die for the good of the Republic, Antony -- given permission to speak at the funeral on condition he not blame the conspirators -- proceeds to systematically undermine Brutus through devastating irony, repeatedly calling the assassins "honourable men" while presenting evidence of Caesar's generosity, humility, and love for Rome. He produces Caesar's will (promising money and parks to every citizen) and displays the bloody, stabbed corpse, whipping the crowd into a murderous frenzy that drives the conspirators from Rome. The speech is studied in rhetoric and political science courses worldwide as a masterclass in indirect persuasion. Marlon Brando's delivery of this speech in the 1953 Joseph L. Mankiewicz film is considered one of the greatest moments in cinema history. The opening line has become a standard cultural reference for any public address, frequently parodied and quoted.
questions.cardFront: Friends, Romans, countrymen, lend me your ears

questions.question: Is Shakespeare's 'The Winter's Tale' a comedy, tragedy, or history?
questions.explanation: The Winter's Tale is technically classified as a comedy but is more accurately a romance or late play, written around 1610-1611, set in the kingdoms of Sicilia and Bohemia (which Shakespeare controversially gives a seacoast, though the real Bohemia is landlocked). The key characters are King Leontes of Sicilia, his wife Hermione, their daughter Perdita, King Polixenes of Bohemia, his son Florizel, and the rogue Autolycus. The jealous Leontes accuses his pregnant wife Hermione of adultery with his best friend Polixenes, causing Hermione to apparently die and their newborn daughter to be abandoned on the shores of Bohemia; after a famous 16-year time jump, the lost daughter Perdita has grown into a shepherdess who falls in love with Polixenes's son, and the play's climax features one of Shakespeare's most theatrical moments -- a statue of Hermione comes to life, revealing she has been hidden alive for sixteen years. Famous lines include the most celebrated stage direction in all of drama: "Exit, pursued by a bear." Based on Robert Greene's prose romance Pandosto (1588). The play's dramatic tonal shift from intense tragedy to pastoral comedy baffled early critics but is now seen as a hallmark of Shakespeare's late experimental style. The 16-year gap in the middle, bridged by the character Time as a chorus, was revolutionary for its era.
